methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Pre-Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $6M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Solideon is a pre-seed robotics and manufacturing company founded by Oluseun Taiwo that has raised $6.5M to date. The relevant peer universe comprises 9 private robotics and manufacturing firms—including 1X Technologies, Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, and Bedrock Robotics—plus one public comparable (277810.KQ). However, the narrow peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables with similar valuation tiers to support a robust peer-median valuation.

Given data constraints, the stage-progression method is the applicable triangulation approach. Using $6.5M in total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a standard 2.5x step-up multiplier from pre-seed to the subsequent financing stage yields an implied valuation of $16.25M. This method reflects typical pre-seed venture progression patterns where companies approximately 2.5x their effective capitalization upon advancing to seed or Series A.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $14M to $19M, centered on $16M. This range incorporates a ±15% sensitivity band around the stage-progression result to account for execution risk and company-specific factors at pre-seed stage. Key uncertainties include the absence of narrow-set peer comparables, which prevents validation via market multiples, and the typical opacity of pre-seed financials that limits revenue or growth-rate normalization. Any valuation remains contingent on substantiation of the technical and commercial milestones underpinning the stage-progression assumption.
